steps.dialogue: In our warmup we found that two bars divided into [event:69740cc7a7aca14b] different numbers of [event:69740cd96a76149d] pieces showed the [event:69740d05f539ae62] same amount of the [event:69740d1eb1ece270] whole bar [event:69740d31f4b1af13] shaded. Now let's look at that same [event:69740df2134bcf29] idea, but this time with the [event:69740d73a434417b] fraction names. These two bars are divided differently, one [event:69740e1bf0e84236] into halves, one into [event:69740e4a507572d6] fourths. Check if they show the same amount or different amounts.

prompt.text: Are they the same or different?

step.dialogue: Let's take another look. Compare the amount of space shaded in both bars.

metadata.description: Overlay or alignment guide appears showing the shaded portions

step.dialogue: You're working on it. Remember: [event:697b92a93d37be8f] both bars are the same size. Check if the [event:697b9357c13f9e36] shaded amounts match, they should cover the same space.

metadata.description: Animation shows bars overlapping to prove equivalence

step.dialogue: Let me help you with this one. The top bar has [event:697b95aa76836029] 1 out of 2 parts shaded, that's half. The bottom bar has [event:697b95e9f059a4a9] 2 out of 4 parts shaded, also half. Different numbers, same amount.

on_correct.dialogue: yes! [event:697413789c79f181] [fraction numerator=2 denominator=4]two fourths[/fraction] equals [fraction numerator=1 denominator=2]one half[/fraction]. They [event:6974164d3996b987] show the exact same amount, just written differently.

steps.dialogue: You found that [event:6974183c4d9d559d] [fraction numerator=1 denominator=2]one half[/fraction] and [fraction numerator=2 denominator=4]two fourths[/fraction] show the same amount. When different fractions show the same amount like this, we call them [event:6974188de2533df6] [vocab]equivalent fractions[/vocab]. Equivalent means equal, the [event:697d154db1b86173] same amount.

So [event:6974192ab0613858] [fraction numerator=1 denominator=2]one half[/fraction] and [fraction numerator=2 denominator=4]two fourths[/fraction] are equivalent fractions [event:697419d984b82437]. They're [event:697419e82e57b874] equal. Let's find more [event:69741af640346849] equivalent fractions.

Here's [event:69741b17d222598b] [fraction numerator=1 denominator=2]one half[/fraction] again at the top[event:69741b39985e647b]. Which bar below shows an equivalent fraction, the same amount as [event:69741b8f10a7eb00] [fraction numerator=1 denominator=2]one half[/fraction]? Click the equivalent bar[event:69741bd3c9988bbe] .

prompt.text: Click the bar that is equivalent to [fraction numerator=1 denominator=2]One half[/fraction].

step.dialogue: [event:restore_snapshot_prompt_start] Almost. [fraction numerator=1 denominator=2]One half[/fraction] means exactly half. find the bar that matches, the other bar with half its parts shaded.

step.dialogue: [event:restore_snapshot_prompt_start] You're getting there. The key is: [event:697d21380417f64d] [fraction numerator=1 denominator=2]one half[/fraction] means exactly [event:697d21d2a8f16ce4] half the bar is shaded. Count the parts, which bar matches the bar on top and has exactly half of its parts shaded?

[event:restore_snapshot_prompt_start] Here, let's walk through this together. [event:697d223c819866d2] [fraction numerator=1 denominator=2]One half[/fraction] means half the bar is shaded. The middle bar has [event:697d228872e179f4] 6 parts total and [event:697d247b9a85ff60] 4 are shaded, that's more than half. [event:697d25ab231d765e] The bottom bar has [event:697d25ebbd5fdad7] 6 parts and [event:697d25feda9dd3a9] 3 are shaded, that's [event:697d273151c4859c] exactly half. So [event:698381e56f573725] [fraction numerator=3 denominator=6]three sixths[/fraction] equals fraction numerator=1 denominator=2]one half[/fraction].

on_correct.dialogue: [event:restore_snapshot_prompt_start] Good, you found another one. [event:69741c76a56811f8] [fraction numerator=3 denominator=6]Three sixths[/fraction] also equals [event:69741c952dc8e2d9] [fraction numerator=1 denominator=2]one half[/fraction].

steps.dialogue: So far we've found that [event:69741de428ecfdc7] [fraction numerator=1 denominator=2]one half[/fraction] equals [event:69741eb8a7f2c0f7] [fraction numerator=2 denominator=4]two fourths[/fraction] equals [event:69741e996d148572] [fraction numerator=3 denominator=6]three sixths[/fraction]. They all show the [event:69742036269e4532] same amount even though the numbers are [event:6974209a0e930570] different. Another pattern is emerging.

Our pattern only works because our bars are all the [event:697420cae46ebac8] same size. We can only say fractions are equivalent when they're parts of the same sized [event:6974212beeddc2b5] whole. [fraction numerator=1 denominator=2]One half[/fraction] of a granola bar, and [fraction numerator=2 denominator=4]two fourths[/fraction] of a school bus, aren't equal, because the wholes are different sizes. But [event:6974214e007a155b] [fraction numerator=1 denominator=2]one half[/fraction] and [event:6974222c2c64c7d0] [fraction numerator=2 denominator=4]two fourths[/fraction] of the same kind of granola bar are [event:6974224462420d53] equal. The [event:697421e1277471f0] whole has to be exactly the same size for fractions to be equivalent.

Let's look at our pattern. [event:697423ca59ec7ea7] [fraction numerator=1 denominator=2]One half[/fraction] equals [fraction numerator=2 denominator=4]two fourths[/fraction] equals [fraction numerator=3 denominator=6]three sixths[/fraction]. They all show half[event:6974241988009808]. Now, how many eighths would equal [fraction numerator=1 denominator=2]One half[/fraction]? Click on parts to shade them.

prompt.text: Shade the number of eighths needed to show [fraction numerator=1 denominator=2]One half[/fraction].

step.dialogue: Let's look at this problem again. Half of 8 parts means shading 4 parts.

step.dialogue: Not quite. Try this: to show half, shade 4 of the 8 parts.

metadata.description: Parts shade one by one to 4

step.dialogue: [event:restore_snapshot_prompt_start] I'll help walk you through this. We have [event:697b9ab8161920f1] 8 equal parts total. To show [fraction numerator=1 denominator=2]one half[/fraction], we need exactly [event:697b9aece51fbc86] half shaded. Half of 8 is 4, so we shade [event:697b9b24649b68a7] 4 parts. That makes [event:697b9b71d6ad33b1] [fraction numerator=4 denominator=8]four eighths[/fraction], which equals [event:697b9b96a719233e] [fraction numerator=1 denominator=2]one half[/fraction].

on_correct.dialogue: Correct! [event:697936921ac5d901] [fraction numerator=4 denominator=8]Four eighths[/fraction] is also [event:6983d609fb1eabc1] [fraction numerator=1 denominator=2]one half[/fraction]. You're seeing how our pattern extends[event:6983d62f009c1ed1].

steps.dialogue: [event:697424988aac7c4f] [fraction numerator=1 denominator=2]One half[/fraction], [fraction numerator=2 denominator=4]two fourths[/fraction], [fraction numerator=3 denominator=6]three sixths[/fraction], [fraction numerator=4 denominator=8]four eighths[/fraction], [event:69791ea633c3bb8b]  they all show half. The pieces get smaller, so [event:6979267cbbac66af] we shade more pieces [event:697d32fc3616ad8d], but the total amount of the [event:697926af543a0957] whole bar that's shaded stays the same.

[event:69792f194b407a82] For fractions to be equivalent, they need to show the [event:697934582101b010] same amount of the same sized whole. All our bars are the [event:6979349e08e6647a] same size, so when they match like [event:6979342f8e68ef67] this, we can be sure they're equivalent.

[event:restore_snapshot_prompt_start] Let me show you. [fraction numerator=1 denominator=2]One half[/fraction] goes right [event:697b9e0dd5a01656] here at the halfway point between [event:697b9e72c33708a5] 0 and 1.

step.dialogue: [event:restore_snapshot_prompt_start] Let's work through this together. Starting at 0, we count fourths: [event:697d3c4038abb8b2] [fraction numerator=1 denominator=4]one fourth[/fraction] is here, [fraction numerator=2 denominator=4]two fourths[/fraction] is [event:697d3ccaa8d3ea54] here,[event:69838d9edbf8263a] right at the [event:69838dc38aba1ae5] halfway point, exactly where [event:697d3cf50affbcde] [fraction numerator=1 denominator=2]one half[/fraction] is. When fractions land at the same position, they're equivalent.

on_correct.dialogue: Right, [fraction numerator=2 denominator=4]two fourths[/fraction] lands at the [event:6973fdea688f8eae] same point. That's another strategy for proving when [event:6973ff203a2893de] fractions are equivalent.

steps.dialogue: Nice work. Whether we use bars or number lines, equivalent fractions will always [event:6979451bc0316a95] match. Bars and number lines are different ways to show the same concept[event:69794558f3218988] .
You've identified two strategies to prove fractions are equivalent: bars and number lines. Now let's learn how to create equivalent fractions ourselves.
Here's [fraction numerator=2 denominator=3]two thirds[/fraction]. Now watch what happens when we split each third in [event:6979395abb642532] half. This is how we will create equivalent fractions.

Follow along as I work. Each shaded third becomes [event:697949612c59f7f3] two shaded sixths when I split them. See? I had [event:697949a82bd24082] 2 thirds shaded. When each third became 2 sixths, my [event:69794af635b8e58b] 2 shaded thirds became [event:69794b1b6f1f8d99] 4 shaded sixths. The amount stays the [event:69794ba6688f3885] same even though we've added more pieces. Now, let's try with a new bar[event:69794c4cf59ec778] .

You're working on it. You can think of it this way: each third became [event:697ba11c623cc06d] 2 sixths when split, so 2 thirds becomes 4 sixths.

[event:restore_snapshot_prompt_start] Let's work through this together. [event:6983a08a7d024c3a] Each shaded third becomes [event:697ba1979fcc898e] 2 shaded sixths when we split. We had 2 thirds shaded, so that becomes [event:697ba2012b7eb3bb] 4 sixths shaded. The amount stays the [event:697ba2565f78013f]same, just with more pieces. [event:697ba294f662e14d] [fraction numerator=2 denominator=3]Two thirds[/fraction] equals [event:6983a171f5d02736] [fraction numerator=4 denominator=6]four sixths[/fraction].

on_correct.dialogue: You've got it. [event:69793ab9751271bf] [fraction numerator=4 denominator=6]Four sixths[/fraction] equals [fraction numerator=2 denominator=3]two thirds[/fraction]. You're creating equivalent fractions [event:69839f59b9b173ad] now.

steps.dialogue: [event:697942601f2bb394] Hopefully that helps you see our new pattern.
